question: What are common failure modes in multi-agent systems?

authority_order: arXiv or preprint papers when proceedings are unavailable
benchmark pages, datasets, repositories, or implementation docs
engineering case studies from primary teams
secondary surveys only when they directly support the claim
discouraged_sources: source aggregators that do not add primary evidence
require_direct_support_for: cost, latency, recovery, or reproducibility claims
recommendations about when an engineering pattern is preferable
source_quality_policy.inference_policy: Engineering inferences are allowed, but they must be labeled as inferences or recommendations and must cite the source facts they are inferred from.
source_quality_policy.weak_source_policy: Weak sources should be rejected during source triage when an authoritative replacement exists. If a weak source is unavoidable, cite it only for the narrow claim it directly supports and flag it in the citation audit.

citation_contract_policy.required_answer_rule: Every important claim bullet or sentence in the Answer section must include at least one literal raw https:// URL in the same line. A Sources section alone does not satisfy claim-level citation support.
citation_contract_policy.rejected_source_rule: URLs from rejected sources must not be copied into the final answer, evidence table, citation audit supported claims, or trace citations.

answer:
## Answer
Common failure modes in multi-agent systems are dominated by coordination, role/hand-off, and verification problems. A trace-based empirical study found 14 failure modes grouped into system design issues, inter-agent misalignment, and task verification, which matches that pattern. https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657

Direct evidence also shows that multi-agent setups can add communication and coordination overhead, and that naive handoffs can confuse the receiving agent or waste tokens if context is passed too broadly. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2 https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/multi-agent/handoffs

### Required aspect coverage
- coordination overhead — covered: surveys note significant communication/coordination overhead in large decentralized MAS, and official docs warn that context management and token costs matter. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2 https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/multi-agent/index
- role confusion — covered as an inference: the survey contrasts unordered cooperation without fixed role assignments with ordered cooperation using assigned roles and expertise. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2
- error propagation — covered: polling/aggregation can amplify shared misconceptions because model errors are correlated. https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06612
- consensus without correctness — covered: more agreement does not reliably mean more truth in unverified domains. https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06612
- evaluation difficulty — covered: failure attribution is hard because MAS traces are nondeterministic, natural-language, and partially observable. https://openreview.net/forum?id=kLLYJ6Bm7n

## Sources
- Survey: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2
- Framework docs: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/multi-agent/index
- Framework docs: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/multi-agent/handoffs
- Empirical failure analysis: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
- Consensus / truthfulness study: https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.06612
- Benchmark for failure attribution: https://openreview.net/forum?id=kLLYJ6Bm7n

## Limitations
- 'Role confusion' is not always named that way in the literature; here it is inferred from explicit role-assignment and handoff ambiguity evidence. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2
- The evaluation evidence is strongest for failure attribution/debugging difficulty, not for every possible evaluation metric in multi-agent systems. https://openreview.net/forum?id=kLLYJ6Bm7n
- These sources are mostly about LLM-based multi-agent systems, so the answer is best read as evidence for that subfield rather than all multi-agent systems.

subquestions: Which multi-agent failure modes recur across surveys and empirical studies?
Which modes are directly supported by primary papers, benchmarks, and framework docs?
How do these sources map to the required aspects: coordination overhead, role confusion, error propagation, consensus without correctness, and evaluation difficulty?

queries: survey multi-agent LLM failure modes coordination overhead role confusion error propagation consensus without correctness evaluation difficulty
official documentation multi-agent framework roles handoff context engineering overhead
empirical failure analysis multi-agent LLM systems taxonomy verification misalignment
consensus not verification multi-agent truthfulness aggregation correlated errors
rationale_per_query: Find a primary survey that summarizes common MAS challenges and failure patterns.
Find official framework guidance on multi-agent coordination, handoffs, and context costs.
Find an empirical study that classifies failures from real traces.
Find a primary source on false consensus / aggregation without correctness.

candidate_sources.title: A survey on LLM-based multi-agent systems: workflow, infrastructure, and challenges
Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
Consensus is Not Verification: Why Crowd Wisdom Strategies Fail for LLM Truthfulness
Seeing the Whole Elephant: A Benchmark for Failure Attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (secondary page)
Polling LLMs raises agreement but not truth; aggregation fails without an external verifier

accepted_sources.title: A survey on LLM-based multi-agent systems: workflow, infrastructure, and challenges
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Primary survey; directly discusses coordination, roles, communication overhead, and scaling challenges.

accepted_sources.title: Multi-agent - Docs by LangChain
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Official framework docs; directly explain why multi-agent systems are used and the context-management overhead they introduce.

accepted_sources.title: Handoffs - Docs by LangChain
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Official framework docs; directly warn about context confusion and token cost when passing subagent history.

accepted_sources.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Primary empirical failure analysis; provides a trace-based failure taxonomy and categories of failure.

accepted_sources.title: Consensus is Not Verification: Why Crowd Wisdom Strategies Fail for LLM Truthfulness
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Primary empirical paper; directly studies agreement vs truth and correlated errors in aggregation.

accepted_sources.title: Seeing the Whole Elephant: A Benchmark for Failure Attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
accepted_sources.why_accepted: Primary benchmark paper; directly addresses why evaluation/failure attribution is hard in MAS.

rejected_sources.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (secondary page)
rejected_sources.reason: Secondary summary/discovery page; replaced by the primary arXiv paper.

rejected_sources.title: Polling LLMs raises agreement but not truth; aggregation fails without an external verifier
rejected_sources.reason: Secondary summary/discovery page; replaced by the primary arXiv paper.

rejection_reasons: Used only as discovery leads; not primary evidence.
Summaries added no extra authority beyond the original papers.

evidence_table.claim: Large multi-agent systems incur coordination and communication overhead; the survey explicitly flags message-delivery overhead, transmission efficiency, bandwidth, timeliness, and scaling coordination as challenges.
Role confusion is best understood as an ambiguity problem: the survey contrasts unordered cooperation without fixed role assignments with ordered cooperation based on assigned roles and expertise.
Polling and aggregation can amplify shared misconceptions because language-model errors are strongly correlated.
Consensus can rise without truth: in unverified domains, more samples and aggregation do not reliably improve correctness.
Failure attribution is particularly challenging in LLM-based MAS because traces are nondeterministic, natural-language, and partially observable; a benchmark is needed to diagnose failures reliably.
A trace-based empirical study found 14 failure modes grouped into system design issues, inter-agent misalignment, and task verification.

followup_needed: A source that explicitly uses the phrase 'role confusion' would reduce inference, but the current role-assignment evidence is sufficient.

contradiction_audit.resolution: No direct contradictions were found. The sources differ in terminology, but they converge on the same broad failure families: coordination/communication cost, role/hand-off ambiguity, shared-error amplification, false consensus, and hard-to-debug evaluation.
uncertainty_notes: Role confusion is inferred from explicit role-assignment contrasts rather than named verbatim in a source.
The evaluation benchmark mainly supports debugging/attribution difficulty, not a complete theory of evaluation metrics.

weak_citations: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44336-024-00009-2 — used to infer 'role confusion' from role-assignment ambiguity rather than an explicit named failure mode.
